textauszug: courtyard as a means of humanizing the tenement blocks in his Tusschendijken settlement (1919)’, 2 he overlooked the fact that the ﬁrst enlarged green courtyards were already in place in Berlin, Vienna and London in the 1890s. Moreover, this reform was not due to one of the avantgardist heroes, but to the previous generation. Furthermore, it was not overcome by avantgardist development, but ﬂourished until World War II—as will be shown in this study. An early and fundamental study into ‘large court blocks’ was provided by Bjo¨rn Linn in 1974, published only in Swedish and therefore with little international dissemination. 3 With a selection of Scandinavian and international examples, he proved the importance of this type of urban housing in the 20th century. Shortly afterwards, Philippe Panerai, Jean Castex and Jean- Charles Depaule published their seminal study,De l’ıˆlot a` la barre (1977, recently translated into English), which aimed to critically review modernist housing estates and carefully promote the advantages of reformed urban blocks in the context of the post- modernist re-appraisal of urban spaces. 4 However, thanks to its title and its speciﬁc selection of historic examples, it conﬁrmed Gropius’s thesis of the general historic evolution ‘from the block to the row’, which still dominates interpretation of the development of modern housing in the early 20th century. More recent overviews, which one might expect to be informed by the broad wave of historiographic re- appraisal of traditionalist approaches, also repeat this modernist interpretation. In Modernity and Housing (1993), Peter G. Rowe seems to deal with modern housing in general, but again simply ignores all attempts to reform urban blocks and to build modern dwellings in a deliberately urban setting. Only avantgardist approaches, mostly inventing a green setting for housing, are considered to be modern, not the more reformist and urban approaches which were also being used in the battle against poor housing conditions under the banner of modernity. He even explicitly comes to the conclusion that: ‘the courtyard form, however, essentially remains a premodern housing type’. 5 While this conclusion is entirely in keeping with his one-dimensional selection of exam- ples, a broader view will prove the opposite: that even in the 20th century the perimeter block was the predominant form of urban building, only shortly interrupted by slab and tower block experiments. Again, in the recent anthology by Barbara Miller Lane on Modern Domestic Architecture (2007), the topic of 20th century urban tenement blocks is entirely absent. Modern domestic architecture is reduced to detached houses in garden suburbs, modernist slab estates and tower blocks—the tradition of modern tenement buildings following a perimeter block typology is eradicated from history. 6 The opposite is W. Sonne / Progress in Planning 72 (2009) 53 –14956 Fig. 1. Walter Gropius, diagram ‘from the block to the row’, in Das Neue Berlin , 1929. 2 Giedion, S. (1946). Space, time and architecture. The growth of a new tradition (p. 525). Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press. 3 Linn, B. (1974). Storga˚rdskvarteret. Ett bebyggelsemo¨nsters bak- grund och karakta¨r. Stockholm: Statens institut fo¨r byggnadsforskn- ing. 4 Panerai, P., Castex, J., & Depaule, J.-C. (1977). Formes urbaines. De l’ıˆlot a` la barre. Paris: Dunod; recently translated into English: Panerai, P., Castex, J., Depaule, J.-C., & Samuels, I. (2004). Urban forms. The death and life of the urban block . Oxford: Architectural Press. 5 Rowe, P. G. (1993). Modernity and housing (p. 168). Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. 6 Miller Lane, B. (Ed.) (2007). Housing and dwelling. Perspectives on modern domestic architecture. London and New York: Routledge. Figure 1. Walter Gropius, Competition Entry for the Chicago Tribune Tower, 1922. Source: Walter Gropius Archive, Busch-Reisinger Mu- seum, Harvard University. Reproduced with permission. 32 GHI B ULLETIN SUPPLEMENT 2 (2005) B auhaus Was ist Bauhaus? Was ist der Grundgedanke? Wichtig Künstler wie Wassily Kandinsky und Paul Klee lernten am Bauhaus. Dieser einfache und schlichte und zugleich Bildhafte Name steht in erster Linie für eine Schule. Gerade, rechter Winkel, Glasfassaden und rechter Winkel. War etwas Neues. Der Grundgedanke des Bauhauses war nicht länger zwischen den bilden den Kunst, also den schönen Künsten und der angewanten Kunst, dem Kunsthandwerk zu unterscheiden. Versönung der Kunst und Kunsthandwerk zu einer neuen Disziplin, dem Industriedesign. Metall, tischlerei , theater , tanz, töpferei , weberei , Schön funktional und innovativ und serienmässig hergestellt werden können. Die kunst soll in den alltag einziehen. Kunsttheorie, entwerfe prototypen . einfache und klare ästhektik mit neuen materialien wie metal und glas o hne verzierung und schnörkel . Dem geist der anvangardbewegung , geometrische abstrakte kunst wie piet mondrian und dem russischen konstruktivismus . Architektur als königsdissziplin ist die architektur , beispiele : haus am horn , praktisch, funktional, grosszügige fensterflächen , 1928 hannes meyer , radikalisierung , einfacher und funktioneller ware für die masse , einfach schön und funktional.
